title: 2026-06-24 2026/06/24 - SSC (Part 2 of 2)
displayTitle: 2026-06-24 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2026-06-24 2026/06/24 - SSC (Part 2 of 2) [Part 2]

chunks.text: Oof.

It's healed. So we got-- I think we've got one submitter. Um- Thank you Michael. Yeah, we've got one submitter and then you, if that works. Yeah. Fantastic. I'm sure Perry first. He's online. So feel free to come up whenever is comfortable for you. Wonderful. Thank you. We'll make sure we get these chairs out the road. Just

as a matter of interest, how do you get in here? Um, I don't remember. Do you go through- But through the lifts So we go over to the bee hive, around- Yeah... woodwook tree, yeah. Right around. Hi, boss. Hello.

I have to come and say hello. Boss wants me. Boss wants me. Along will come- I think we're on the way now. Well, so riveting this morning with our- Oh, he's going to come up. Oh. Oh, welcome back. I think he lives in the ground. All right, we're, uh, we're open. All right. Well, uh, welcome back everyone.

We are continuing this morning's hearings in front of the Social Services and Community Select Committee. And, uh, first up, we have Joshua Perry, who's appearing via Zoom. I don't have my notes on either. Are you sure I didn't pay that? Maybe I did it.
Put it this way, that's not a problem.
It's fine? I think, um, you just need to turn your microphone on camera. Oh, there you go. We can see you. Great.

We had image there for a second, now we don't. We only have image or sound right now. Thank you. Oh, there we go. Uh, there we go. We can see you and hear you. Welcome, Josh. Thank you. Since 2010, I've, I've been living on my own, uh, independently, and my-- Um, I've had to rely on family support.

This legislation, um, if it's passed or on the Monday, because at the moment currently I'm having to, to, um, pay my public transit with the administration cost for the, um, individual coming out. I wonder, has this legislation, um, uh, uh, taken away, um, the youth parts of disabled people and, uh, and the rights of the youth

convenience, which was ratified in 2008. Um, today leaves no clear path for families under this legislation, um, which also breaches the Health Act and the Human Rights Act from 1991. Um, at the end of the day, I'm just trying to make my own way in the world.

Um, my recommendations are the Ministry be a community of disabled people to look at this legislation and the impact on families, as well as a clear 50-year plan to ensure the viability and the sustainability of the system and the national bit that it has taken.

Yes. Thank, thank you. If you have any questions, please feel free to ask me. Oh, thank, thank you, Josh. I appreciate it. Um, anyone got, um, questions for Josh? Uh, you seem to, you seem to have made yourself clear, so th-thank you, thank you for making, um, both your written and, and oral submission, Josh.

Appreciate you taking the time to do that, and, um, and it's something we'll, we'll take into consideration as a committee. So thank, thank you very much for doing that today. No problem. Thank you. Kia ora, Josh. Thank you. Thank you, Josh. See you. The next, um, submitter is, um, Michael Nolan in person with this.

All right. Uh, welcome, Michael. Uh, thanks for appearing in front of the committee this afternoon. Um, so we've got, uh, five minutes with you, and when you're ready, the time is yours. Wonderful. Kia ora. Ko Michael Nolan toku ingoa. Recently moved from the small town of Franz Josef Glacier on the West Coast to Christchurch with a lived experience of Becker Muscular Dystrophy.

I'm submitting on the proposed Disability Support Services Bill on some real concerns I have and the implications on fu-future support for disabled people across Aotearoa I worry about the expectation that families will take on more responsibility without clear or proper financial assessment. From where I stand, or should I say, flip, that indicates a shift in responsibility more onto whanau, which is not realistic.

This bill, in its current form, represents a fundamentally flawed approach to disability support and will lead to negative outcomes for disabled people across Aotearoa. It's an attempt to provide unrealistic certainty, particularly around the continuation of funding for family carers. However, it does so by significantly weakening rights, protections, and accountability.

Whanau supports need to be backed by a fair and just system that provides all the support needed for not only disabled people but also their carers. The overall direction of this proposed bill is not supportive of disabled people and should not proceed. A central and deeply concerning feature of the bill is the removal of access to established complaint mechanisms, specifically the Human Rights Commission and the Health and Disability Commissioner.

This effectively removes independent avenues for redress without providing any clear or equivalent alternative. This is absolutely unjust. It strips disabled people of a key protection and leaves a significant gap in accountability. If a disabled person experiences poor treatment, discrimination, or harm under the system created by this proposed bill, there is no clear path to justice, support, or legal representation.

Disabled people will be left without adequate and essential protection. This proposed bill fails to adequately recognize or address the risks that can exist within family care situations. While many whanau provide high-quality, culturally appropriate, and deeply committed care, not all environments are safe or supportive. There are circumstances where neglect, coercion, or misuse of funding can occur.

In removing external complaint pathways while also expanding reliance on family-based care, the bill does not demonstrate how disabled people will be safeguarded in situations where care arrangements are not working or are actively harmful. Without a clear independent mechanism for monitoring intervention or advocacy, this creates an unacceptable level of risk for some of the most vulnerable people in our communities.

Through lack of meaningful engagement of disabled people, this proposed bill undermines trust and is inconsistent with the principles of participation and partnership. An emphasis on fiscal risk and cost containment, including a suggestion that increased funding may not be feasible or access to disability support services may need to be reduced, signals a shift away from a rights-based approach.

The use of a three-year transition period provides absolutely no reassurance on outcomes. There is insufficient clarity about what any long-term system will look like. There is a real risk supports will diminish, protections weaken even further, and disabled people will continue to face increased uncertainty.

Asset or means testing should not be a consideration in disability support because it ignores the real needs of disabled people and creates another unfair and unjust barrier. Introducing such significant legislative change without a clearly defined and rights-based end state is not acceptable.

The bill is not fit for purpose. There has been no allowance for people to think it through, talk with others, and provide meaningful feedback. Where is the co-design? Where is the thorough and engaged consultation with disabled people informed by the lived experience, grounded in rights, dignity, and inclusion? I sincerely ask decision-makers to think very carefully about what is being proposed here, because real consultation takes time.

Time to understand, time to think, time to respond. Legislation must be co-designed. Disabled people and their whanau and supporters need to be fully involved, fully informed, and genuinely listened to by the decision-makers, not after the fact, but as part of shaping what happens next.

Thank you very much, Michael. Well said. Thank you for making that submission and sharing your perspective on the bill. Appreciate it. Thanks so much. Safe travels back home. If that's where you're headed. Good to have someone from Franz Josef up in Parliament. Thank you. Good place. Yeah. Right, our next submitter is Kaye Jones, I believe, in person. We have Kaye?
Yes. Okay.

Welcome. We have you for five minutes this afternoon. Welcome. Kia ora. Ko Kaye Jones ahau. I'm a former policy advisor with questions about the lack of legislative drafting in this bill. But I don't want to take the time with that, so I just want to touch on two points from my submission.

I'll give you the choice. Do I start with opportunities or risks? Let's go opportunities. Okay. I thought that would be the case. Mm-hmm. So if the committee is wise and recommends sending the bill back for co-design with people within the community to strengthen it and to clarify matters, then there's the opportunity to look more at the training and job opportunities for disabled people that are currently missing out.

Because I think all of us around this table would like disabled people to be as independent as they can and earning their own income- Now, challenges to that include that government agencies do not, as a matter of course, have part-time employment. They do not have a pathway for disabled people who cannot work full-time to be in that. If there could be an across-the-board system, as there have been in the past, for working that way through, then you could actually get more people. Even those people who, some of the submitters

who have cerebral palsy and who have diction issues, some of them are very able writers and can use computers. And with the assistance given with AI these days, there could actually be a lot of opportunities. But at the moment, there's no resourcing going into it. So I think that whole thing of working with the communities and Whakaha Ministry to develop ways to actually enable disabled people to look after themselves more or fund it, that would be great. Now, the risks, we've heard about some of them before, but I'll just mention two names,

Danella Knox and Janine Albury-Thompson. Both of them were loving mothers to autistic daughters who were incredibly difficult to manage, and both of those mothers killed their own daughters because of the stress and the burnout and their inability to cope without support.

Now, unfortunately, there are many families who may face similar issues if they are forced into a situation of caring for adult disabled people who are challenging. The last Ministry of Justice survey in 2021, showing comparison of crime between on adult disabled versus non-disabled populations, 6.5% of disabled adults experienced violence by family members compared to 2.2% of the general public.

Now, that whole stress of caring for somebody is a challenge. I do touch in my bill of the risks of sexual assault on disabled women that happens within communities. That is heightened by pressures to put people in a situation where they're expected to care for family and there's no assessment.

If you look at the Family Violence 2018 Act, there is definitions in that act and protections that are there that are very difficult for some disabled adults to access. So I'd want there to be closer looks at the risks to the disabled people.

Anyway, I'll stop in case there are any questions because I just have so many examples. I might start with one. Yeah. And it's been one of me sort of burning away for a while, but I've been waiting for just some to leave some time to ask it. So there's a lot of focus on family- Yeah... and sort of building a perception that family's going to be forced to do this. But if I look at clause eight, it says, "The Ministry must take into account the following principles.

Families, whanau, and other culturally recognized family groups, where appropriate, have responsibility in the first instance for the well-being of their members." So it's where appropriate, it's not that they'll be forced to. I'm sorry, I'm a former public servant. Where appropriate will be dropped to the bottom of the list. Where convenient is going to be a much more pragmatic situation.

For example, I have an adult child who has a minor health issue, not a major one. If something happened to me and my partner, my brother would be expected to pick up that because no criminal record against my brother, has a reasonable income. But actually has no skills, knowledge, or connection with my adult child because we're not a close family.

There are lots of loving Maori families who will have that connection, and there's lots of Pakeha who do not. I'm sorry to be culturally sort of biased on that, but appropriate will be assessed on the basis of can they financially afford it, and is there a criminal record against them? It won't be on the nature of do they have the skills, the inclination, the willingness.

And that's as a public servant, I know what those types of tick boxes have. So I think there needs to be choice. It shouldn't be on the case of the state deciding who will be the support. Any disabled person should be having their say. If that has to be assisted because they're non-verbal, there are still communication ways.

Most of the people that we're talking about still are able to communicate. We've heard from a number of people sort of, I've been watching yesterday and today, people can communicate their wishes, and they have that right. Don't treat disabled people as second-class citizens. I don't think it does. But no, thank you for making that submission and your perspective on it. Cheers.

Yeah. Inākuhi. Thanks, Kaye. Any other questions? There are more, but we're just going to have to wait till the next person hopefully leaves some time for- Okay. And if we're talking about the billions that's been spent, it's not been spent in all regions evenly, which I know has been a question you've been asking people because the Enabling Good Lives is not rolled out across the whole motu. It's only rolled out in three regions with a start in a couple of other areas. So if you look at what the government got for $35 million-

Sorry, so we'll-... in Immigration New Zealand- We'll have to- You know... move to the next speaker We want effective money. Yeah. Inākuhi. Thank you. Right. Our next speaker is, or next presenter is Magenta. Also on piece, I believe. Welcome. All right. Well, you've got five minutes with you. Fire away when you're ready.

Yeah. Well, I've got a lot to say and a lot of time, so I'll be direct about why this bill is concerning. The Supreme Court's 2025 ruling opened a pathway for family carers to seek employment status and rights for the disability support they provide. This bill closes that pathway. It extinguishes the 40-plus claims already in progress, blocks any future employment-related claims, and bars carers and disabled people from bringing related complaints to Employment Relations Authority, the Employment Court, the Human Rights Commission, or the Health and Disability Commissioner.

This is not a minor technical fix. It is Parliament overriding a court decision specifically to stop carers from being recognized and paid as employees. Legislation that permanently forecloses a court-recognized legal pathway for tens of thousands of disabled whānau carers deserves a genuinely co-designed, well-being centered process, not fast track. I'll focus on family carers and what losing this pathway means for them, for the people they care for, and for the country's books.

Carers are already underpaid and overworked, propping up an overburdened healthcare system. Closing off their path to employment rights locks in more unpaid and unrecognized care, which is dangerous for both carer and disabled person. Rana Itel's 2026 study, applying theory of caregiver stress to 15 years of Australian panel data, found carers reported higher happiness than non-carers when providing under six hours of care a week, but lower happiness once care exceeded 11 hours a week. The highest burden group, who provided 20-plus hours

weekly, showed the clearest decline in well-being. This bill, by foreclosing the path to paid recognized work, pushes more carers into that high hour, low recognition bracket rather than out of it. Australian research backs this up. Carers of family members with severe illnesses are more likely to have reduced paid employment, higher out-of-pocket costs, and greater exposure to economic decline and financial stress. Underpaid carers also carry a heavier cognitive load, which compounds poor well-being, meaning stressed, fatigued carers and the people

they care for are both more vulnerable to accidents. Carers in this position have neither the time nor the resources to look after their own health. This bill is being sold as fiscal restraint, but Infometrics 2022 analysis for Alzheimer's New Zealand, Carers New Zealand, the Carers Alliance, IHC, and MSD shows that restraint is an excuse, not a saving. In this, they noted that one in seven New Zealand adults, 14%, is an unpaid carer. Additionally, there are already 7.9 unpaid carers for every practicing nurse and 9.6 for

every personal care assistant or disability support worker. A bill that entrenches unpaid family labor as the default rather than a recognized employment relationship will push that ratio higher still. Infometrics estimates carers lose between 1.5 and 2.3 billion in employment revenue annually from reduced hours and lost paid work, and that the government loses 540 to 769 million a year in tax revenue as a direct result.

By legislating away the Crown's status as employer, this government isn't avoiding the cost. It's choosing to keep it sitting with families instead of recovering even a fraction of it through a healthier, larger tax-paying workforce. The 2021 State of Caring survey found many carers are already in financial distress, less satisfied with life, and critically don't feel valued. This bill sends them a clear message: that they are a fiscal liability to be legislated around, not workers deserving of basic employment rights.

Infometrics had some recommendations that point in the other direction completely. Paid leave, tax credits, KiwiSaver contributions, increased carer support payments, and access to the supported living payment. They estimated that meaningful financial and well-being support for carers would generate 20 to $40,000 per carer per year in net national well-being benefit for comparatively modest public investment.

Schemes that were already in motion, such as Mana Whaikaha, enhanced individualized funding, and Enabling Good Lives, were flagged as practical starting points for exactly this kind of support. But it's worth the committee noting that this government's track record with these very schemes is a cautionary one. In March 2024, Whaikaha abruptly restricted what flexible funding could be used for, citing a budget overspend with no consultation, cutting carers' access to things like respite.

It took a sustained community consultation and nearly two years of hardship before those restrictions are finally being unwound from April, with no apology and no commitment to restoring what families lost in the meantime. This is the same pattern that this bill risks repeating. Cut first under fiscal pressure, consult only once the damage is already done. This government has cut so many jobs and continues to squeeze the poor and disabled to cover deficits created by giving tax breaks to landlords and tobacco companies.

I'm asking that the committee do not let this bill pass in its current form, not only for the harm that it does to disabled whānau and their carers, but because employing these carers properly is a net economic gain, not a cost, at a time when the government should be creating jobs, not legislating away the few legal pathways that carers have just won. I'm happy to send through any references should you like to read those.

Yeah, we'll-... definitely. Maureen, quick question. I note your comment around government overriding the court decision. Mm-hmm. That's actually the system that we live in, in this country. The Parliament, government, um, writes the legislation. If it's not clear, the courts make a decision, then we've got to clarify it. It is not up to the courts to set legislative, um, priority.

I agree. There does need to be a framework, but it needs to be co-designed and well-being based, not based on numbers and budgets that people out there are going to be making. All right. Thanks, Magina. Thanks for making your submission this afternoon. Thank you. Thank you very much. Our next submitter is Jasmine, uh, appearing via Zoom.

I'll send you a sign. You'll be presenting in NZ Sign- Jasmine, um, do we have a New Zealand Sign Language interpreter as well? Or is it just- Yes. We've got interpreters online. Oh, great. Thank you. Just wait for a moment there, um, Jasmine. We just want to make sure we've got the interpreter online, uh, so we can see them as well.
Excellent. All right. Welcome, Jasmine. We have now our New Zealand Sign Language interpreter, and the time is yours when you're ready.
Okay. Thank you.

Yeah, so tena koutou, tena koutou katoa. Thank you for this opportunity today for me to come and present. I'll ask the interpreter who's using sign language today, and I wanted to mention that because New Zealand Sign Language is one of the official languages of New Zealand.

So that's why I wanted an interpreter here today to be able to turn my signs into voice for all of you to understand. Okay. Thank you. I oppose this bill. It is blatant disrespect. It dishonors Whānau Ora, Tangata Mai Kaha, Māori Turu, deaf and disabled people, the United Nations and Disabled People's Organizations Coalition, which the government is mandated to listen to.

The coalition commission and publish reports via Donald Beasley Institute on this very topic of disability support services. The proposed law, under guise of trying to avoid the responsibility of being an employer, is rewriting and reframing disability support services in a way that does not uphold deaf and disabled people's rights.

If it is anything, it is an admittance that the current system is failing us and that our government is in breach of UNCRPD to propose this after Minister Upston stated last year that New Zealand is an international leader in disability rights.

The government deciding what is disability support services legislation without proper partnership with deaf and disabled community, that's not the way. It's obviously not a coincidence that this government does not honor Palestinian people's rights and Aotearoa's obligations as signatory to the Geneva Convention.

It's not a coincidence that the government is dishonoring Te Tiriti o Waitangi and breaching the rights of trans, intersex, non-binary people. Government does not get to dictate to us what our gender is or what our supports are. It is our right to determine that for ourselves.

I want to show you these words and art by Ollie Costello, because I feel it's relevant to this bill, and it's relevant to other bills that are currently up for legislation. It's called "Letting Go of the Gender Binary," and it says, "The gender binary has its roots in colonization.

It was used as a form of hierarchy and control. It was and is used. Yet when we look back at history, we can witness long lineages of human societies with gender expansive people. All over the world, we see a dynamic spectrum of plants, animals, and other living beings embodying the same truth, that we exist on a planet of beautiful and diverse possibilities.

No two people ever are the same. Why should our genders be, or our supports?" For example. "When we let go of gender binary thinking, it frees us all to be our true selves. Self-determination is a human right. When we practice using someone's pronouns, when we affirm gender expansive existences, we create networks of care. We create a safer world for all." It is directly relevant to this bill as well.

So how can our government do the right thing? Roll out the bill. Start from scratch. Build legislation with us. Implement these recommendations that are made. Let Donald Beasley Institute publish this on behalf of the DPO coalition. It has all of the recommendations that are from us deaf and disabled people.
Toitū teriti, free Palestine. Ka mihi. Any questions, anyone? All right. You seem to have made your points, Jasmine. Thanks for presenting to the committee this afternoon. Thank you.
All right, the next submitter is Deidre in person.
It wasn't updated when I was given. Right. I think that's perfect. Sorry, didn't realize. That's all right. Next.
Deidre. Kia ora. Uh, kia ora. Welcome. Hold on.
All right, we have, uh, five minutes with you this afternoon, so when you're ready, come on right in. Gee. Mm.

Yeah, so kia ora. My name's Deidre. I'm a financially solo caregiver for a beautiful, intelligent, funny person. Uh, we receive disability support services through individualized funding. Um, professionals have told me I support an incredibly complex situation.

And what I've experienced is that the compartmentalization and inflexibility of our various health, mental health, social welfare, tax, and education systems is actually disabling. These systems could enable, but they're not fully designed for the breadth of impairments, and nor are they adequately funded.

I work in the community and social services sector, and I find the system's opaque and impenetrable. I can't figure it out for myself. Um, so yeah, it just has far-reaching impacts. Um, in terms of this bill, I'm concerned about the human rights aspects.

Um, I think this bill is at odds with the Universal Declaration on Human Rights and the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. I'm incredibly concerned about clauses 8 (2) and 11 (3) of the bill. Um, the way they're written, I see that they could enable means or asset testing in the future. I would really like them removed.

The, um, the r- you know, the way this bill, you know, we had such a short time to respond. There hasn't, in my opinion, been adequate consultation with the disability community. And so I cannot trust, I cannot trust the executive to, um, introduce secondary legislation that enables our people.

Um, and I guess what I'd like to say to you is that, um, you know, I receive this individualized funding. I'm part of this disability support services system. Um, but I just feel like it's this malfunctioning cog in a failing system. Um, so we get some funding we're entitled to, but it fails to provide anything that we really need.

And, but we still need it, and it just has this incredibly huge administrative burden for a very small amount of money. But we still need it. Um, I was told by a professional that we should be receiving more individualized funding, um, comparable to what other families receive. Um, that professional advocated to the NESC.

We were discharged out of that service. Um, and then it took over a year of me continually advocating. And, you know, this timing's great because I've just been told that actually after all of that, um, our situation doesn't provide enough alignment with the rules to receive more funding anyway. Um, and then this cog, it misfits with other parts of the systems to create even more failure.

Um, we've timed out, we've aged out of services that, you know, have been starting to help. Um, we've not been able to access services because the consent thresholds were too demanding for us. Um, we've had mental health and disability services argue over responsibility to, to support us.

Utterly failed by the education system, and incurred a technical debt because government systems don't align. So it's exhausting. It shouldn't happen. Um, I'll stop there. I've got more, but yeah. Thank you. Maree Mills. Thank you. Um, tena koe, Deidre. Welcome. Thank you for your kōrero and your, um, and your submission. You used the word trust, which is, um, just pretty fundamental. And when that isn't there, um, and all of the, the lines or that you've def- that you've defined, will more funding change that?

Will, will-- Yeah, will that-- If we threw more money at you, would that be the thing that undoes all of these things that you've just described as nearly opaque and impenetrable? Um, it's not just about money. Of course it's not. But actually adequate funding- Yeah... you know, funding that people deserve to live a full life, um, would build trust. And, you know, the ability to actually build relationship with professionals. Having the professionals adequately funded, funded. All of that would help. But I just think, you know, everything

needs a full redesign. Um, uh, I, uh, I didn't have time to go and check this out, but I have heard that Enabling Good Lives was not fully implemented. Mm. So I'd love you to please go and just check that out. Mm. You know- Make the system function. The systems function Thank you.

Mm. Thank you. Yeah, and on that, I was thinking this a few times yesterday, but sort of interested in what you're sharing with us because two years ago, the sector was funded with $2.4 billion. There's now been a $1.5 billion uplifts in the two years since then by this government. So it's a big uplift. So it's like it's interesting to hear that people are seeing this as a defunding measure when a $1.5 billion uplift in the last two years.

Well, I guess when it comes down to the personal level, it feels very defunded. Yeah. Thank you very much for your submissions this afternoon. Appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you. All right, next on here is Lonnie and- Yeah.
Or should we move to Rangimarie?
Rangimarie, you may come forward.
Good afternoon and welcome. Thank you. All right, well, we've got five minutes with you this afternoon. Time is yours, so fill as you wish. Thank you.

All of you here today, thank you for allowing me to speak on this bill. Rangimarie. Sophie Jolly, " Allenau Kituwaicato." Kia ora. I'm grateful to be here today to speak on behalf of my whanau, although I must admit, I would much prefer to be speaking in consultation and not with the threat of legislation looming over my every word, so I'm a little bit nervous.

Go for it. It's a friendly space. Good. I'm coming today to speak as a mother, as a daughter, and as a wahine Maori. I'm a caregiving parent to an incredible, brave, and audacious 16-year-old kohine. And as a caregiving daughter to a 65-year-old retired nurse and incredible nanny.

I'd like to take this opportunity to make visible to you my invisible life and our invisible home. I am working and on call 24 hours a day, seven days a week, for years and years on end. And I will spend the rest of my life in this role until I'm physically unable to do so.

As a naturalized carer, I live with my mother and daughter, who are both significantly paralyzed. My mother 16 years ago by a rare cancer, my daughter by a cold when she was just nine years old. At the time of my daughter's illness, I was a successful, working single mother with a career, plans to buy a house, plans to foster children, and plans to travel the world with my daughter when she was old enough.

Overnight, our worlds turned upside down. I won't get into the details of that event or that period of our lives, but I will say that every day in the seven years since then, it has been my absolute privilege to be everything she needs because I am a bridge between her and the world. My job is to meet her needs and somehow curate independence in her future.

I'm on a three to four hourly routine of cares every part of every day, including being on hand throughout the night when she's in pain. Most nights, I get about five to six hours of broken sleep. I then wake up every morning and I start again. Between these cares about two years ago, I miraculously managed our lifestyles to the point where I could work again.

And fortunately, I found a job that allows me flexible hours and the ability to work from home, but these jobs are very few and far between. And as unsustainable as chronic burnout is, this is the only setup I'm afforded. I'm a naturalized carer, so there's no nurse to replace me. No system is monitoring my wellbeing.

No one is covering my sick days or approving leave requests. No one is authorizing my skills or my ability to do this job well. No one is wondering whether or not I can provide adequate care. No one is giving me a bonus for accurately predicting a severe medical event and taking action to avoid it. No one has me even on a register and asks what happens when I'm not there.

No one is monitoring them or me, and no one feels like they're looking at us. And no one's also asking who will take care of my daughter when I'm unable to. No one's asking who she wants doing her cares when she's old enough to consent to that. And no one is asking how that person will be paid for their skills and labor.

No one's asking how a 38-year-old woman is supposed to survive working 24 hours a day, seven days a week, for years and years on end with zero financial support to offset the ever-increasing cost of living. And no one has yet realized that in some whanau, a naturalized caregiver might exist for two people, and that person might also have to be the breadwinner because our welfare system doesn't cover the cost of living.

Mm-hmm. Especially when you have complex healthcare needs. So my biggest deficit is actually time and not money, and I've used that time today to come into this room so that you can see me and other parents like me who feel like they've been wrung out like a damp rag that nobody ever replaces Because the issue that I'm finding is that this bill doesn't actually represent our needs. It represents an avoidance of liability.

And so when that whanau won that Supreme Court case, we cried. Mm. I cried. I felt like someone was going to see me. Mm. And then all of a sudden this bill came and I felt completely invisible again. I'm aware that I'm running out of time, but I just want you to know that whatever decisions you make, I hope that if anything ever happens to anyone in you or your whanau- Mm ...

the decisions you make today in this space will help to do something to offset that pain and that exhaustion, and that you don't find yourselves and your whanau in this situation. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you for coming and sharing that with us. Very articulately. Cheers. Be well. Thank you. No, it's fine.
All right. And next speaker is Jane Curran.

Good afternoon and welcome, Jane. You've been here all day. Well, a lot of it. I think at least- Thank you. Cheers. You've drawn the short straw, you're going to have me the whole time. So- And I've just found out I'm only getting five of my 10 minutes so, I won't- No. Everyone's getting five minutes... I won't follow on with further pleasantries. No, I'll- All right... sorry, can we pause that for one second? Yep. Just start again, please. Just so I could, you probably heard this morning that we as a committee because of the constitutional separation of

powers between the courts and- Oh, I'm very familiar with the constitutional separation of powers. Don't worry, I will not be naming any names. It's just any active court case. And yep. I'm not... Yes. Understood. Far away. On that basis, I'm a independent disability advocate, and no one in this country knows more about this issue than I do. Bluntly.

So, I've thrown away the script. You've got my five minutes worth of submissions, written submissions. I'm going to direct this particularly at the Act and the New Zealand First party, because you two could actually make a difference. I'm here to sort of wipe away some of the myths that I've been hearing here for the last couple of days.

I'll start with the topical issue of separation of powers and the explanation about the role of the courts and the role of Parliament. Your role here is to scrutinize the bill. Legislation. Not to defend it, but that's all I've been hearing, is defending this bill. Am I aware that the Crown has put $2.5 billion into the disabilities in the last few years?

Yes, I'm aware of that, but that's only because you had to, and it's only because you've gutted disabilities in the last 30 years. Between 1999 and 2016, the disability budget actively went backwards. In 1992, the disability budget was $1 billion. Index adjusted, that was just under $2 billion.

So you can see we're already streets behind because what are we at the moment? At 4 billion. The reason I'm pointing this at New Zealand First and Act is number one, Act are a helpful on disabilities. And two, you two actually have the capacity to sway the-- because this is clearly, five minutes worth of scrutiny for each person means that the decision has already been made.

I'm not at all convinced that there's any scrutiny going on here today. So the billion dollars that we were getting back in 1992 pales. The budget, $2.5 billion in the last two or three years means nothing. You will never catch up on the decline in the gutting of the disability budget over a period of a good nearly 20 years, off the back of family carers. The exploitation of family carers is what has made the disability sector tick over. I've had to shove my fist into my mouth listening to some of the organizations sitting here before you today.

The Disabled People Assemblies put up witnesses against the family carers in the Employment Court. So, there has been a huge polarization and it has been the family carers. They're the people I want you to walk away from- Yeah over the next few days and discuss.

I don't want you looking at the organizations. I want the people who have sat here before you today and cried. That man I sat with today, I've never met him before in my life. But I can tell you I've met dozens like him. I take telephone calls at two o'clock in the morning about suicide. I talk about suicide and murder like other people talk about the weather. That are the family carers that I work with, and I have worked with solidly since 2014.

Because I have seen what a disgusting system we have. And frankly, this committee, your predecessors, you have a lot to answer for. We shouldn't be sitting here today in front of a social services committee and community committee. We should be sitting in front of the education and workforce committee. So we're before the wrong committee to start with.

The fact of the matter is, the Supreme Court are not meddling in disability policy as has been conveyed by government. And I'm genuinely disappointed because I have to say, I will say about Minister Upston, she is the one minister who's come along in the last 20 years and said, "Something's not right here. Why are the people who need the most supports not getting it?"

So that is why we're sitting around this table today, because unfortunately, as is the way, having started off down the right path, she has been diverted, and I should imagine it will be by our lived experience policy advisors who've said, "Let's pull this together. There's no framework for disabilities." That is, in modern-day parlance, BS. There is a disability framework for Africa.

We've got the Disability Person's Community and Welfare Act from 1975. We've got the Health and Disability Safety Act. We've got the Public Health and Disability Act, replaced by Pae Ora with its dis- The only definition of what disability support services is, and that is about to be trashed by the new definition to be replaced by this bill. We have the Resident Services and Disability- we have the Residential Services and Disability Support Services Act.

We have standards, we have the Crimes Act, we have New Zealand Bora. What the Supreme Court did, respectfully, because I strongly suspect people do not understand this sitting around the table, was all it did was it looked at government policy and it said, "You are not following the law." That is all it said.

The court cases aren't over. We're back before the Employment Court in September. That is why this is being rushed through. That is why, because you're terrified you're going to have 20, 30 odd people in the ERA, some who have sat there for nearly seven years. They haven't rushed off and thought, "Oh, ripper, I'm going to file in the ERA and make my first million." They haven't done that since the Supreme Court handed down their decision. They did it seven years ago.

They have been sitting there waiting, in good faith, in a holding pattern while the Fleming matter went through to the Supreme Court. And every government, of all hues, no one government rises above the other in this area. Every government of all hues has done their best to delay everything. I've been involved in court cases for a long time, and I can tell you, I have never...

Your lawyers, $5 million. Is that a waste of money? No, that's not a waste of money. But trying paying out a few family carers. If you were to pay out everyone sitting in the Employment Relations Authority at the moment, I doubt if it would come to 50 million. There's 20, 30 families. It wouldn't come to 50 million.

But if it came to 50 million, so what? You guys are sat back. You've got policy. You've had the minister, fair dues to her, she's gone off and she actually has, of the three reviews she did, I would call two of them genuinely independent. Ironically, the one that's quoted the most is the one I would say is least independent, but that's neither here nor there.

It still raised very valid points. The fact of the matter, the other two, the Link report and the Nash report, along with the NASC report that sits at the back of the complex care submissions, they actually reveal the real picture. Hundreds of millions of dollars of procurement fraud in disability support services over the last 27 years.

Now a navy boat sinks a year or so ago, $100 million. We rush out within days, we've got a judicial review happening of it. What happens about the procurement fraud? Nothing has gone out to tender in the last 27 years in disability. That is why we have such a shocking system, because we have a system made up of patronage, conflict of interest, you name it.

And that is where Minister Upston is dangerously close to identifying what the real issues are. And that is why you have the, what I refer to as nothing about us without us band appearing before you, because they are terrified their conflict that they have managed to so carefully protect for the last how many years, is about to be blown open by this act. This bill is the wrong bill. If the minister is genuine about bringing clarity, transparent and fairness, you would go back and you would look at all of those bills that already sit there and you would say, "Okay,

how can we turn this into something that actually works for everybody?" You wouldn't bring along another bill so that it can be put with all the pile of other bills that will get breached. I can guarantee you right now for nothing, I have read your bill, and I will spend the next 15 years, you can take this to the bank, I will spend the next 15 years in court fighting. So it won't cost you $5 million, it won't cost you $20 million, it won't cost you the $50 million that you might get away with paying out the people currently in the ERA. People haven't rushed off to sign up

to the ERA. Why not? Because you've heard the parents. They say, "I just want to be a parent. I don't want to be a 24-hour exploited slave." But that is what they are. And the supreme irony is that we are sitting here today while in another select committee room, they're discussing the Modern Slavery Bill.

And the Modern Slavery Bill definition of exploitation, if you pass this bill, it will make this government the biggest breacher of the Modern Slavery Bill, because every aspect of that exploitation and servitude, the government will be breaching by passing this bill.

So I'm preaching to the converted. There are some people who are against it, some people who are for it. And those people who are for it, I genuinely accept that you mean well and you believe that this will make a difference. But I can tell you right now for nothing, you know nothing. You need to scrutinize. Get out there and scrutinize. Sitting here and trying to convince us that lots of money has been put into a system that is utterly broken.

Not one of those parents who sat in front of you and cried has been able to say, "Oh yeah, no, I remember. I did get a few lazy 100,000 out of that billion dollars over the last year." Jane, I have let you go 10 minutes. I think we have to draw close there. Yeah. But thank you for your passionate submission. I agree. And can I just end by saying, I hate being referred to as passionate. I am not passionate.

Yeah. To be passionate suggests you love something. I hate what I do, and I'm angry. So take that to the bank. Angry submission. You know I do. Thank you. You know I do. I don't mind. Next matter, or do we have Lily? We could always go to the next matter in person and circle back. Okay, cool. Yep. All right. Let's do Lesley. Is Lesley available?
And that's been very-
She's come from- What's your name? Ah. Geez. Can we move? Yeah. The same submissions follow. Very good.

Welcome, Leslie. I hear you come from all the way from Motueka. Is that correct? That's correct. That's impressive. Well- It's been a long day. I can imagine. I don't see the sun that you brought with you, but Thanks. But welcome. We have you for five minutes- Okay... so fire away when you're ready.
To the cohort of chair and members of the committee- You know thank you for the opportunity for making the decision on the disability support services. My name is Leslie Jackson.

And I live with progressive sorry. Just when you're ready. I live with progressive multiple sclerosis and have done for the last 15 years. Enabling Good Lives originated as a community-led initiative, strongly supported in action by the government under Tariana, sorry, I can't pronounce the name, Tariana Turia in 2011.

Mm. It has since received bipartisan cross-party endorsement as a guiding framework for disability support system transformation in New Zealand. Governments of different political stripes have continued to reference and fund its principles. In March 2025, the government made an abrupt change to flexible disability funding without meaningful consultation with the disability committee. The impact were immediate and severe.

Across New Zealand, parents and whānau describe being left in shock when respite care for disabled was suddenly removed. These were not minor adjustments. They would directly contradict the principles of Enabling Good Lives the successive governments had previously endorsed.

What was taken was not fully restored until the 1st of April 2026. The disability community was assured that this unilateral cut would not happen again, yet here we are considering a bill that risks repeating the same mistakes. Clause 82A of the bill states that family groups have primary responsibility for their disabled relatives where appropriate.

This wording raises concerns for many of us. I ask the committee to carefully consider the evidence of overseas. A recent UK study by Professor Gavin Giovannoni, Chair of Neurology, Bartholomew's Hospital and Queen Mary University London, examined domestic violence experienced by women with multiple sclerosis.

Among 275 married women with MS, 53 reported psychological abuse, 63 economic abuse, 43% physical abuse, sorry the percentages, 20% sexual abuse. In New Zealand, approximately 5,000 people live with MS, of whom 3,750 are women. Many disabled people already rely on family members and can be especially vulnerable when they have limited choice on who provides that care.

Forcing greater reliance on family carers without guidance cho- choice is not empowering. It can expose people to risk, erode independence, and place unsustainable pressure on families. Disabled people must retain the right to choose their support and their carers, including the ability to use flexible funding for paid support when that is what their informed preference is.
I recommend, I urge committee to amend the bill.

Disabled people keep the choice and control of their supports. Disability funding remains generally flexible and protected, and protected from sudden top-down restrictions as has happened. Ensure the principle of Enabling Good Lives are embedded in a legislation, not undermined by it.

We have lived experiences and evidence from both New Zealand and overseas that can help get this right. I ask that you return to the table with the dis- with the disabilit- disabled community, and listen carefully. Design a system that truly enables good lives rather than placing new risks and burdens on the very people it's meant to support.

I thank you for your time. Thank you very much. Appreciate you coming and making a submission, coming all the way from Motueka. So it's a long way to come. It's not an easy journey, so thank you- It is. Thank you... and sharing that story. And thank for your brief written submission as well. You're welcome. Yes. Thank you, Leslie.
Um, do we have Louise? I don't- We're going to stick to the plans. Okay. Talk through the lot? One line at a time

Oh, good afternoon, Lily. Welcome. Kia ora. No, kia ora. We can see you. We can hear you loud and clear. So five minutes is yours to fill as you wish. Thank you. Tena koutou katoa. My name is Lily Parehall Butcher, and I strongly oppose this bill. As I explained in my written submission, I believe this bill will cause suffering for disabled people and their loved ones.

I'd like to talk about the notion of care. I've thought about it a lot as I helped care for my grandmother in the last years of her life. In order for care to exist, so must trust. To have a good quality of life, disabled people must be able to receive care from people they trust. Requiring disabled people to rely on family support before being eligible for state support takes away choice and agency over these decisions. Would you want to rely on a first cousin you haven't seen in 20 years to provide care for you?

That's what this bill could require disabled people to do. At the same time as this bill would take away disabled people's choice over who might provide them with care, it also refuses to support the whanau who would be required to act as caregivers. Denying family caregivers rights as workers and financial compensation for the work they do will undermine their ability to care for their loved ones.

Even disabled people who would be eligible to apply for state support under the system proposed by this bill would likely have increased difficulty accessing the support. The Citizen Advice Bureau's 2020 report into digital exclusion in government services highlighted the challenges disabled people face when applying for support, as well as barriers they experience when trying to meet their obligations or claim entitlements.

Application processes are already often inaccessible, and disabled people specifically may require specialized software or equipment to navigate digital application processes. Adding further layers of bureaucracy and potentially time pressure into this process will increase the administrative burden on disabled people to apply. As such, those who would be eligible for support may miss out, and even those who are able to access support may suffer other harms as a result of the process. For instance, people who experience stress-induced

seizures or chronic fatigue would likely experience negative health outcomes as a result of applying under the proposed system. In my written submission, I was speaking mainly from my experience as a caregiver, but I'd also like to speak from my personal experience as a disabled person. To me, I think this bill is a deeply shameful piece of legislation.

It is a transparent attempt to make disabled people pay for the consequences of the Crown's own actions. The attempt to foreclose any possibility of caregivers being provided with support to care for their loved ones. And beyond that, there is also a lack, I think, of belief in the ability of disabled people to live good lives.

Restricting the options that people have over who provides them with care or how they support themselves will impact people's options and opportunities in their lives. One of my close friends moved to Wellington for university in order to be able to study what she wanted to study. And because of her cerebral palsy, which produces stress-induced seizures, if she had to work full time as well as study full time, she would not be able to do both, and she would not been able to afford to study if she had not been able to receive

disability support. And so she would have been forced to stay at home and be looked after by her mum, and she's just completed her degree and has in fact won a really prestigious scholarship. So she's had all sorts of opportunities and has contributed to our community in Wellington in all sorts of ways since she arrived here several years ago. And she has been able to do that because she has had a bit of choice over how she lives her life.

I think forcing people to have a lack of choice over how they live their lives really restricts people's opportunities- Right... and forecloses on their potential as people. And this in particular, I feel could breach the Articles 19 and 28 of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Disabled People.

I think the committee should think very carefully before proceeding with this bill. I think it should be rejected. And in particular, the clauses related to means testing and the fact that there are no specific elements of the-- The fact that eligibility requirements be left up to subsequent legislation, I think is very suspect. And I think that it should follow the guidelines as suggested by CSS Disability Action.

Thank you for listening. Thank you very much, Lily. Thanks for sharing your experience and also for the support you had for your grandmother as well. So both experiences. Appreciate that submission and the recommendations you've made. We'll take those into consideration. Thank you. Very much.
All right. The next submitter we have available, I believe, is Kate. So can we bring Kate online?

Good evening, Kate. Thanks for being available a little bit early. If you're- No problem... you're ready to go ahead, we'll jump into it. Sounds good. Tena koutou katoa. Thank you for this opportunity. I work at Te Wananga Aronui o Tamaki Makaurau or AUT in the Critical Disability Studies space.

I've worked as a physio, and I live with complex health conditions myself. I want to really acknowledge the wisdom shared by users of disability support services and carers, and I really hope that the committee here can feel the weight of their concerns and let that guide some of your decision-making and scrutiny of this bill.

While I don't use disability support services, I, like all New Zealanders, have a stake in this. None of us, none of your voters or constituents, know when we might need ourselves to rely on those services. So to respond to your questions, I'm concerned that this bill positions disabled people and their carers as economics burdens on the state, on the budget.

There's a long legislative history of this in New Zealand and in other English-speaking countries, right back to the time of the Industrial Revolution. But legislation and funding arrangements can and do look different in overseas territories, for example. Yesterday, I noticed the committee mentioning, and today again, the additional funding, billions of dollars in allocation.

But my understanding is that covers increased costs rather than to enhance services, and it's a very small proportion of our total government annual budget. In addition, it was said that some disabled people can and do work, and that workers should contribute economically to their own support services use. But working or not, disabled people have lower incomes and higher costs, have poorer health outcomes, have poorer educational outcomes, have poorer quality and often inaccessible housing, and have poorer access to transport

into our public spaces. Those outcomes are not a consequence of the disabling condition that they themselves have, but are reflective of having been underserved by government and society for generations. On their reading of this bill, teachers and health professionals that I know who are also carers of dependents have told me they worry that sustainable employment will be even harder for them.

Combined, all of these issues mean that earning a sufficient income with enough surplus to cover any impairment-related costs or equipment, or caregiving costs would be almost impossible. So what would I like to see? I'd like to see this bill completely rejected by all parties in the Select Committee.

The disability community feels under attack with very real safety concerns. For example, about tamariki traveling to school, in alignment with changes to the total mobility scheme subsidy, and with sudden political and funding decisions being made about them or without them. These actions have breached trust and good faith between the government, between ministers, and the disability community.

In addition, practicing health professionals have told me they despair the system they now work in. They're struggling to turn up to their job because of wait lists and funding constraints and unfilled frontline positions. They keep on working, often at their own expense of their own health and well-being and families. Or they leave and know that their position will remain empty, further restricting disabled people's access to rehabilitation and equipment or adaptive technologies, particularly in rural remote areas that are difficult to staff

already. So my proposed solution, I'd really like to encourage all of you to use the Select Committee process as an opportunity to hear, to learn, and to start again, to develop new and better policy in relation to disability support. I'd encourage each of you to remember your ideals of freedom, of justice, of care for others, of the reason that you became politicians, and to imagine what would be possible if you put those ideals first.

Thinking of your disabled constituents and voters as interesting people, who, if their needs are well met, can live ordinary and meaningful and wonderful lives. I'd like to see carers, family carers, be acknowledged and appreciated financially and legislatively and beyond. I would like you to be good stewards of our taxes and to notice, for example, which private companies are profiting from the current status quo, because it's certainly not disabled people or their carers.

I'd like you to work together to develop great world-leading accessibility legislation where diverse access needs are met as default rather than as afterthought. I'd like the government to properly fund Whakaha. I'd like a transformation of our fragmented health and education and welfare and justice systems so that disabled people don't have to spend so much time and effort educating professionals and traversing systemic gaps and failures themselves as individuals or as family units.

I'd like you to take a critical disability lens to each and every piece of legislation and policy in government and within your own political party, so that disability issues become front and center and embedded as due process. I'd like you to consider how to meet the cultural and language needs of diverse communities, including tangata Whakaha Maori, Whanau Ora, Pacific disabled peoples and their aiga, and migrant and refugee communities, and weave this into explicit disability-related policy with humility and awareness.

And I'd like you to explore how the Crown may offer respectful kawenatanga while respecting tino rangatiratanga and Te Tiriti o Waitangi. So in summary, I'm concerned that this bill is deficient. I encourage you all to reject this bill, and I suggest a new start, working towards Aotearoa, New Zealand, being a world leader in the disability issue space, drawing on our common values for decency and for fairness.

So to do that, I suggest that the minister and this government needs to undertake a process of reflection and of learning, including that of our awful, horrific disability history, and a listening to this community and a rebuilding of trust. I'm very happy to continue in conversation regarding any of this, now or in the future, and I'm very happy to take questions. Thank you.

Thank you very much, Kate. I think we are a bit over time, but I'll let you get through it all. But look, thank you for coming online and making a submission this afternoon. Appreciate it. Thank you very much. Do we have...
We will finally move the remaining motion, too. Shall we suspend for five minutes and just see if we can get it?

Good afternoon, Barry. There we're coming up. Hello, Ken. Oh, good day there. Yep. Am I visible? Yes, you are visible, and we can hear you loud and clear. So, welcome. Thanks for making yourself available. We have you for five minutes this afternoon. The time is yours when you're ready. Okay. Thank you very much. Well, thank you very much, Chairman. Thank you for the opportunity to present to you on this bill, which has the potential, I think, to impact significantly on the lives of disabled people in New Zealand.

As noted in my written submission, which you have, I'm the father of a now adult son born with Down syndrome in 1980. So in part, I'm here to speak for him. My concern is how the contents of the legislation will help him and his peers, affect him and his peers, as an intellectually handicapped adult who seeks an independent life with an extended family framework but is unable to support himself because of his disability. So firstly, I'm concerned that the extended nature of the obligation that the legislation seems to provide is that it requires family

members to provide the first tier of support. I have no objection in principle to that. But the definition of family group is very widely drawn. Minister, it seems, will have almost an unbounded ability, it seems to me, reading the legislation, to effectively, and I'm using ACC as an example, but interrogate me and my close family, and also those of other family members, grandparents, uncles, aunties, and even first cousins. And the point I'm making is that goes a long way from the central core family that we're operating under.

The manner in which the rules are set to operate are effectively at large. That are there are no limits within the bill, it seems, on the extent of the intrusion into our wider family relationships with our son and the extent to which those members are able or willing to assist in supporting him. And this intrusion into my widening family group by government, I have some objection to.

I think it's potentially divisive, damaging, and unwelcome in my view, unnecessary. There's other ways of doing it, I think. And such processes will put significant additional pressures on families that because by virtue of having a member with a disability are already under some pressure from society, and that's just the way it is. I know that from my own experience.

So to put it into context, I think we've got to think about these sort of family support environments in two phases. When a disabled person such as my son was at school, the local community and the education system provided support for him. Demands on the family unit were akin to the natural support that any family has, so the support requirements were well known, and they were shared between the system and ourselves as a family. However, when this disabled person becomes an adult, 18 to 20, he or she makes a very different set of demands on his family and the community than doing

than do the regular members of the family who are becoming independent. And natural support for the disabled member in such cases can become at significant cost to the family concerned in terms of time, resources, and opportunities forgone. And don't misunderstand that last one.

People, families have to make decisions around the abilities of the people that are in their family to whether they go overseas, take promotions, do other things. Quite a major factor in our life in this case. Accordingly, the machinery around the natural support sharing, I think needs to be carefully designed and proper consultation with those that are affected does need to occur.

I don't want to be overly critical, but this legislation came on us somewhat as a surprise. I emphasize I don't think it's a ledger-based, cost-accounted, or bureaucratic process. It's one where there's needs discretion, flexibility, and judgment. And the materials in the legislation, the way it's framed, seem to be very prescriptive. I'm an engineer.

I know about performance-based regulation, and it's quite a different level than prescriptive-based regulation. So I was looking for some evidence that there's some discretion and judgment involved. I couldn't see any in the bill that were there. Secondly, the We have a minister at the moment who's indicated to us that she's a supporter of enabling good policy, and that's very good. But future ministers responsible for the implementation of this bill will need to have no doubt about the overarching purpose.

And if we don't set the legislation up right with some avoidance of doubt clauses in it, then the disability support services stream and the system will get compromised because it won't be clear where it sits with respect to families and the obligations of the partnership we're talking about.

I emphasize that there must be a partnership between families because of the bill's purpose and principles and the government agencies involved. It is a relationship that needs to be nurtured. It must be based on trust and experience, and consequently, that's why the consultation issue is quite important.

I think that the MSD minister needs to recognize the significant value of contributions that are made by families, material and other, as they support members with disabilities. I don't know how many of your committee have firsthand knowledge of being in families with this, but it can be very demanding and, in some cases, life-changing. So forms of intrusion of those families has to be handled very carefully.

I ask members of your committee to put partisan politics aside and be brave and act in the interests of all New Zealanders when reporting back on this bill. It is quite an important piece of legislation. It's a complex area. You've got a sector that, to some degree, is already under pressure, and this has got the potential to make it quite a lot more difficult.

I believe you need to accurately record the concerns of families, including disabled people themselves, when you report back to the House on this, thinking and keeping in mind the downstream consequences of something which, if not carefully drafted, could be quite damaging. Thank you very much for your time.

Thank you, Barry. We are out of time, but I sort of want to just get your quick response to this. You raise an interesting point. Now, I didn't draft this, so I have to just guess at the intention of the drafters, but they seem to have drawn a broad definition of family, whanau, and other family groups, as you've identified. I guess they're trying to identify that there are different sort of arrangements in New Zealand. But your point is that it's too broad and creates maybe some unintended consequences.

Do you have any recommendation on how we could narrow that, taking into account- Yeah, I think you've somehow or other got to tier it. You've got to have a situation where you define close family, and that would be parents and siblings, I think. Then you've got a further layer out. If you were going to inquire as to the ability of others to be involved, you've got to investigate, for instance, where that further out group, some of them might be in Australia, they might be all sorts of places.

You've actually got to, I think, categorize that support framework. In some instances, for instance, a next-door neighbor will be closer than a first cousin. I took exception to the first. I think the first cousin sort of thing was just going far too far. I think the siblings is about the limit, and at that point, I think you need to sort of start standing back and think about what families are.

Now, I realize that different cultures have a slightly different approach. I'm just talking about a European-New Zealand situation where Travis has three siblings, and we're all very much in the support network for him. But if we went out to his cousins and things like that, it would be very hard.

All right. Thank you very much, Barry. Thank you for your submission and sharing your thoughts on that, and something that we haven't heard yet. So that's-- Yeah, appreciate it. Okay. Right, our next submitter I believe we have available is Colleen Brown. Yes, she's coming up.
Oh, brilliant.

Good afternoon, Colleen. Welcome. Hello. Thank you very much. I'll just rattle through mine same way as Barry's done his. So thank you very much for the opportunity to address you today. I'm here today as the mother of four adult children, one of whom, Travis, has Down syndrome. I want to make five points.

Point one is I included our son's name in my submission deliberately because this proposed legislation seems to deliberately deny Travis his voice, his agency, and the voice of disabled people, apart from making a written submission to this committee. Travis has a voice. He makes decisions about his life, and we support him to do that. He has rights, and importantly, he has a valued place in our family and in society.

The minister has publicly stated she supports the principles of enabling good lives, and if that is so, the minister can't say, "I value disabled people," and then in this bill exclude their voices from the very legislation that so intimately affects them. You can't have it both ways.

Point two, underlying this bill is the premise that our son, our children's brother, is a cost, a burden. Not an economic unit, and consequently is of a little value. That's the way many in the disability sector have interpreted this bill. It may not be the intent of it, but for us, always fighting for inclusion, that's the message it sends to us.

We as a family have missed out on many opportunities. We've chosen to be actively involved in our son's life and still are. Even though he flats with his mates, I spend at least half a day a week supporting him, and often more. But there is a cost, and it's not just a financial one. Point three, we've always viewed our relationship with the state as a partnership.

We do our bit, the state does its bit. And like all other citizens, we have that relationship. But apart from the fact that our support for Travis has never gone away for 46 years, usually your support for your children dwindles when a person reaches the age of 18 or their 20s. The untie whanau support for Travis is for life.

We expect the state to be there in partnership for its most vulnerable citizens. This bill does not send that message to me. The definition of family, what we refer to as natural supports in the disability sector, in the 46 years I've been involved with many disability organizations and parent groups, I have never met a parent who's not wanted the best for their disabled family member.

Their contributions are huge and should be acknowledged and valued. To have the family listed out in the way it is in the bill seems rather contemptuous of the years of support willingly given to this country by families. Mm. So I ask you, why include that list and at the same time exclude disabled people's voices?

The bill stigmatizes us as a family even more than we are now. Always seeking that extra support from a huge list of people. It's like going around with a begging bowl. Back to the charity discourse. Point five, the very disturbing secondary legislation, the extraordinary powers given to the minister, which might not be invoked by this minister, but it leaves it wide open in the future.

Of particular concern, and no doubt it's been mentioned by many others, is income and asset testing along with decisions around a person's disability. That's very worrying, especially when linked to the definition of family. Are you really going to ask my children or professionals to give up part of their professional lives to be their brother's caregivers?

DSS has historically always been needs-based. This apparent change has never been signaled in any meetings or communications from the government. Some laws do have asset and means testing, but not disability support services. So why introduce it in this bill right now? It's very tough out there in the disability sector.

We are all survivors of, at times, a very indifferent system. This bill has created a huge amount of anxiety. What I do know is, in the 46 years I've been supporting Travis, we've had 17 education ministers, 13 MSD ministers, 14 disability issues ministers, and 20 ministers of health.

And what I also know is that when you've all retired and look back at your legacy in Parliament, think about these two things. Will this bill be a moment of pride for you? And also know that in the disability sector, we know that politicians come and go, but families are there forever. 46 years.

This bill signals a lifetime of having conditional relationships, asking extended families, "Can you, would you support us in any way?" And not knowing how the state would carry on supporting you, if at all. So I ask you in all conscience, where is the hope? Mm. Where is the value? And if you vote for this bill in its current form, is it really, is this the best you can do?

Mm. Thank you, Colleen. We are over time, and I'll just say questions to crack it. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Quick question. Thanks, Colleen. Your third point, to remove all reference to secondary legislation. Now, the minister's rationale for this is that she's creating the framework and then the detail will come later. Are you arguing that it should be the other way around, that the detail should be consulted on out through engagement with disability communities and then written into both the framework in detail interlinked?

The devil's always in the detail. We know this. We've dealt with policies over the years. And so yes, we do need to know what the detail is. It is very disturbing having that secondary layer. We don't know what's coming. We don't know how it's going to be interpreted. And while this m- this minister may have the very best of intentions, five years down the track, 10 years down the track, when you have-- you've got your lovely pensions and you're sitting there thinking about what you did in the past, we are the ones fighting for it. No.

We've got to know now what's what. Mm. Thank you. Thank you, Colleen. I should just say, parliamentarians don't get pensions any different than any other citizen does. Just for the record. But point taken. Thank- thanks for making your submission. You're welcome. You're welcome. Unless you're paying. All right. Do we have- More things.

Yeah. Linda. Okay, Linda. Sorry. Just getting- Oh. Oh, there we go. We can see you loud and clear, and we can hear you nice and clearly as well. Welcome, Linda. Thanks for appearing for our committee this afternoon. The time is yours when you're ready.

Thank you. I have a few points to make, and I'm happy to take questions because you've read my statement. So, first of all, I just want to make the point that families of people with disabilities are some of the very hardest working people in Aotearoa. We often think that farmers are hardworking.

Tell you what, families of people with disabilities work a whole lot more as well as that. And it doesn't stop As families will have told you, there is no retirement age. There is no three weeks off or any weeks off in a year. You can't just step away from it. And families stay connected regardless of what else happens around.

So they know their responsibilities and they don't shirk from them. It's rather disingenuous to suggest that this is a new idea, that families should take responsibility, and it feels rather insulting to families who are there day in, day out, 24/7, regardless of what else happens. I've been a sibling, am a sibling, and a grandmother, and so I can tell you, it is a commitment, a long-term, lifetime commitment, and families live up to that. It's hard work.

As people will tell you, it often means stepping aside from other opportunities and committing to family members. So what families seek is consistency and support from government, and that can be hard to come by. Consistency means knowing what's coming next. When things change often, as they do, that makes life more difficult than it needs to be. So my reading of the purposes of this bill is to limit financial risk to government, to establish a three-year pathway to get legislation framework, and to manage DSS budgets.

Now, I can relate to some of that. It is important. However, I would suggest that it's time to acknowledge that there is a need for increases in funding within DSS budget. It just isn't enough, and it hasn't been enough for some time. So in developing frameworks going forward, this needs to be kept in mind, and I would suggest that families need to have a voice in that, as do people with disabilities.

Because without this being done in a thoughtful way, the out costs will be far more expensive for government. I have seen how when these costs are cost shifted to other parts of government, and they will be, it actually costs a whole lot more.

So investment now and upfront in a meaningful way would be a better outcome, I would suggest. I'm happy to take questions. Mm. Thanks for that, Linda. Appreciate that. Actually, just on that, a sort of question on my mind is, obviously, this is about DSS-funded disability support services.

And I note Section 8 talks about that the ministry or contract provider must take into account other publicly funded sources as well as family and as well as their community. So, I'm just wondering if you can just, from your experience, just talk us a little bit through what the differences between the DSS-funded services are and the other publicly funded services are that are sort of typically accessed.

Well, obviously, education's one of the earlier ones in the life of a child with a disability. When there are artificial boundaries between that, then it can be a case of families trying to battle between these services. And there should be a linking up. So, where therapy is provided in education and not in health or not in DSS, there's a tension there. Those are services that need to link up more.

Equipment services, when they stand alone, make life difficult for families to negotiate. And DSS funding has a look across some of those, but over time, we've created these boundaries between them. I've seen in health where DHB services or Ministry of Health services end up picking up the shortfall when nothing else is available.

And so the cost in health is way more than a cost in DSS. And so again, working together. I know that there's at least 100 people sitting in hospital services now who have a disability and don't belong in a mental health services, but they're in the most expensive place they could ever be. And so we need to work together. If DSS was able to provide the support in the community, health would not be carrying that burden. At the moment, we've cost shifted it into another place, a really much more expensive place.
