Adviser. That was very helpful context, and I thank you for that. And thank you again for making submission this afternoon. Thank you. Appreciate it. Yeah. That's really good. We have Amy. Amy? Great.

Good evening, Amy. Welcome. Good morning. Yep. No, we can hear and see you, so great. The time is yours when you're ready to go. Thank you. So my name's Amy Taylor. Multigenerational disability has shaped my life. I'm a disabled person living with multiple sclerosis, and I'm also autistic. These experiences shape how I move through the world and what I'm showing up here for today. I come from a line of disabled people.

So I grew up alongside my mother, a feisty, unapologetically disabled woman living with advanced MS. In my late teens, I became her carer. This was the late 1990s, when the lack of a lot of disability support services meant that we were put in that position.

That experience has had a lasting impact on my education, my income, and the career choices available to me. It also put significant strain on my mum and I's relationship, which became really unhealthy for a long time. Today, I work three days a week. This is what I can sustainably manage, and if I work more, I enter cycles of burnout and symptom flare-ups that render me completely useless.

I'm also raising two disabled children with complex health needs. They can't attend school full-time, but they're curious, bright, motivated to learn. They want to contribute to society. They just can't do it at the pace that non-disabled people are expected to. And when I look at their future, I feel really worried, because this bill assumes something that my life tells me is not true.

It assumes that families can carry the load. This bill places responsibility for care first on whānau and reinforces ideas of natural supports, but I've lived what that looks like. It looks like children becoming carers. It looks like intergenerational poverty.

It looks like people having to choose between their health and their economic survival. Because when the state steps back, someone else carries the cost, and that cost is already being carried by disabled people and their families. It frames disability support as a contribution, not as a right, not as an entitlement. That matters, because a contribution can be reduced, a contribution can be withheld.

A contribution does not guarantee dignity. But disabled people's lives and wellbeing should not depend on what is left over. It is a matter of human rights. We've recently had Supreme Court recognition that some family carers are workers. It acknowledged the real labor of care.

This bill moves away from that recognition. I have lived that work. I still do. It is constant, it is highly skilled, and it shapes entire lives. I've serious concerns about how this bill is structured. Much of the real decision-making, who gets support, what they receive, and how is left to regulations rather than the bill itself. This creates too much uncertainty.

It means the rules that shape my life can change without meaningful scrutiny and without disabled people being meaningfully involved, and many in our community already feel that consultation has been lacking. Finally, I want to speak plainly about independence. When support systems rely more heavily on family, they assume those families are safe and able to provide care, and that is not always true. Not all disabled people have safe or supportive whānau, and when survival is tied to family or partners, people can be forced into

unsafe or abusive situations. That is a loss of independence. It is a loss of choice, and it is a human rights issue. Disabled people need to be seen as having inherent value. When you're not worrying about how you're going to feed yourself that week, you can use your strengths to contribute meaningfully to communities.

That is what a good support system should do. I want my children to grow up in a system that recognizes their value, supports their independence, and does not limit their future based on how much they can work or how much their family can carry. A disability support services legal framework is needed, but this bill in its current form should be withdrawn and redrafted in genuine partnership.

What disabled people need is certainty. We need our rights, as outlined by the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, to be strengthened, protected, embedded in legislation so that they are not put at risk by changing political parties.

All right. Great. No, thank you very much, Amy. I appreciate you coming and making a submission to the committee this evening and sharing the experience of you and your family. Thank you. Cheers. Thank you. We have anyone else? We have and Natalia coming. Okay, so we've one who couldn't make it and one who we gave them an out to reschedule.

One. One couldn't make it and one we've made reschedulable. Is Amy to come? All right. I think that- That's all. That's all we've got. All right. Well, that concludes us for this evening on the public session. We'll move private. So we'll move to private session now. Okay.
